nativeRange.habitat: Slow-flowing to moderately flowing shallow rivers and streams with silty substrate, tannin-stained water, and abundant biofilm and aufwuchs (the layer of algae, microorganisms, and organic matter that colonizes submerged surfaces) growing on rocks, wood, and plant leaves.

diet.notes: Primary diet is aufwuchs — biofilm, diatoms, and soft algae grazed continuously from hard surfaces and plant leaves. Accepts algae wafers, blanched vegetables (zucchini, green beans, cucumber), and gel foods such as Repashy Soilent Green. Does not effectively consume tough or filamentous algae. In new or sparse tanks, starvation is a documented risk even when supplemental foods are offered, as the species appears strongly conditioned to grazing behavior.

avoidWith: boisterous fast-feeding tankmates that outcompete at feeding

summary: The Otocinclus is a small armored catfish from South American streams, widely kept in planted aquariums for its continuous grazing on soft algae and biofilm. Groups of six or more are recommended, and a mature tank with established biofilm growth is essential for long-term success.
careNotes:
CRITICAL: Otocinclus have been reported to decline rapidly — sometimes within the first two weeks — when added to tanks lacking mature biofilm. The species is a continuous grazer and supplemental foods alone tend to be insufficient in tanks under roughly 4–6 months old. A tank should ideally have visible biofilm and diatom growth on hardscape and glass before purchase.

What is sold in the aquarium trade as 'Common Oto' is typically a genus-level mix of species. O. vittatus (slender body, faint lateral stripe) is most commonly exported, but O. macrospilus (Olive Oto, deeper body) and O. cocama (Zebra Oto, distinctive dark bars) are also sold under the same common name. Care requirements are largely similar across the genus.

The vast majority of specimens in the trade are wild-caught; captive-bred individuals are uncommon. Wild-caught fish are generally more stressed in transit and tend to benefit from quarantine in a biofilm-rich tank before introduction to the main display.

Water quality should be stable. Otocinclus are commonly considered sensitive to ammonia spikes and abrupt parameter changes, so a fully cycled tank is important. Soft to moderately hard water is accepted; very hard or alkaline water tends to be suboptimal.

Copper-based medications should be avoided. Like other members of the family Loricariidae (armored catfishes with reduced scalation rather than conventional fish scales), Otocinclus are commonly considered sensitive to copper treatments.

Otocinclus are not strong jumpers, but a fitted lid is advisable as with most small fish.
breedingNotes: Spawning has been reported in well-maintained aquaria following a condition period on varied foods and a water change to simulate seasonal cues. Spawning follows a T-position similar to corydoras; eggs are adhesive and deposited on plant leaves and glass. Clutch size is typically 30–50 eggs; fry are reported to hatch in approximately 3 days at typical tank temperatures. Rearing fry requires abundant soft algae and fine biofilm — commercial fry foods are often not accepted initially. Breeding is considered difficult relative to other small catfish.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (3.3 cm TL for O. vittatus), temperature (20–25°C), pH (6.0–7.5), hardness (dH 2–18), native basin distribution (Amazon, Orinoco, Paraná/Paraguay, Xingu, Tocantins). Size range widened to genus-level to reflect trade mix. Temperature min lowered to 20°C per FishBase; max consensus at 26°C across three sources.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked temperature (22–26°C), pH (6.8–7.5), gH (7–15 dGH), lifespan (3–5 years), min tank (10 gal / ~38L — adjusted upward to 60L for group of 6+ per controller brief and playbook bias toward larger tank for schooling species). Min group size: 6+ confirmed. SeriouslyFish (404) and PracticalFishkeeping (403) were attempted and blocked; Fishkeeping World used as second cross-check source instead.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-layer. Spawning in captivity has been documented but is uncommon, and requires a mature, algae-rich tank and conditioning on a varied diet. The pair spawns in a T-position similar to corydoras catfish, depositing adhesive eggs on plant leaves and glass. Fry require abundant soft algae and fine biofilm to feed on.
